era_context: Continuation of the Old Kingdom in Egypt (6th Dynasty), the Akkadian Empire under Naram-Sin in Mesopotamia, and the mature Harappan civilization in the Indus Valley. This is a period of high state centralization, monumental architecture, and long-distance trade, though environmental stress (particularly drought in the Near East) is beginning to accumulate.

events.title: Continued Old Kingdom administration — Egypt
events.description: The 6th Dynasty pharaoh continues administrative and military operations. Tomb inscriptions from this period document expeditions to Nubia (Kush) for gold, ivory, and incense, and to the Levant for cedar and other resources. The decentralization of power to provincial governors (nomarchs) continues, a trend that will eventually undermine central authority. Pyramid construction continues but at a reduced scale compared to earlier dynasties.

sources.name: Tomb inscriptions and administrative papyri from Saqqara

events.certainty_note: Year-to-year events are not individually documented; this represents the broader reign context.

events.title: Akkadian military campaigns — Naram-Sin consolidation
events.description: Naram-Sin's military campaigns continue to expand and consolidate Akkadian control. Inscriptions document campaigns against the Lullubi (Zagros Mountains), the Martu (Amorites), and other groups. The empire's administrative apparatus is refined, with Akkadian language and cuneiform script imposed on conquered territories. However, paleoclimatic evidence suggests increasing aridity in the region, which will eventually strain the empire's agricultural base.

sources.name: Akkadian royal inscriptions and administrative texts

events.certainty_note: Naram-Sin's reign is well-attested; specific year-to-year events are not individually dated.

events.title: Harappan trade networks — Indus Valley
events.description: The Harappan civilization maintains extensive trade networks. Seals and weights found in Mesopotamian sites (Ur, Lagash) and the Persian Gulf (Dilmun) attest to regular exchange of goods. Indus Valley products (carnelian, lapis lazuli, cotton textiles) are traded for Mesopotamian tin, cedar, and other resources. The standardization of weights and measures facilitates this trade. No evidence of military conquest or political domination; trade appears to be conducted through merchant networks.

sources.name: Harappan seals found in Mesopotamian contexts (Ur, Lagash, Nippur)
Standardized weights and measures from Harappan sites

events.certainty_note: Trade networks are well-attested through archaeological evidence; specific year-to-year fluctuations are not documented.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence for this year; the broader chronological and interpretive issues noted for -2514 apply here as well.
historiographic_note: This year is not individually documented in surviving sources. The events listed represent the continuation of broader trends from -2514. Year-to-year history is not recoverable for this period; only reign-level or era-level patterns can be discerned.
